serving.description: 1 plate (3 cream croquettes with curry rice)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving consists of approximately 550 g: three deep-fried cream croquettes (approximately 150 g total) served alongside a generous portion of Japanese curry sauce over steamed white rice (approximately 400 g combined).

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from USDA FoodData Central: Croquette (breaded, fried, cream-filled) and Japanese curry rice components; cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy entries for white rice (cooked), curry sauce, and béchamel-filled croquette
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for cream croquette curry rice as a composite dish. Values estimated by weighted component analysis: steamed white rice (~55% by weight, USDA SR Legacy #20045), Japanese curry sauce (~18% by weight, estimated from USDA curry sauce analogue), and cream croquette (~27% by weight, estimated from USDA breaded fried croquette with béchamel filling). Per-100g values derived from weighted blend of these components. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (cooked, Japanese-style) and mixed curry rice analogue
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for cream croquette curry rice. GI estimated at 65 based on the dominant carbohydrate source being Japanese-style white rice (GI ~68–72 per University of Sydney GI Database) moderated by the fat and protein content of the cream croquette and curry sauce components, which slow gastric emptying and reduce the effective GI of the composite meal. Composite GI estimated conservatively at 65 (medium). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 140.25) / 100 ≈ 91.2, rounded to 91.6 using 550 g serving × 25.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g = 140.25 g available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~1705 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium load from curry sauce and seasoned croquette coating; a single serving may provide over 70% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load (~91.6 per serving) driven by large portion of white rice
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise caution with this dish as the large white rice component produces a very high glycemic load per serving; substituting brown rice or reducing the rice portion size would substantially lower postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from béchamel cream filling and deep-frying, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The deep-fried cream croquette contributes saturated fat (~2.1 g per 100 g) and the curry sauce adds significant sodium, both of which are risk factors for cardiovascular disease; individuals with CVD risk should limit portion size and frequency of consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cream Croquette and Curry Rice (3 Pieces) is a Japanese comfort food dish consisting of three deep-fried cream croquettes (korokke) served alongside Japanese curry sauce over steamed white rice. The cream croquettes have a crispy golden breadcrumb exterior encasing a rich, creamy béchamel-based filling typically made with butter, milk, flour, and sometimes potato or onion. The Japanese curry sauce is a mild-to-medium spiced thick gravy made from curry roux, onions, carrots, and potatoes, characteristically sweeter and milder than South Asian curries. The combination creates a satisfying contrast of textures — crunchy croquette exterior, silky cream interior, and soft rice with velvety curry sauce. This dish is popular in Japanese curry restaurants and yoshoku (Western-influenced Japanese cuisine) establishments. Nutritionally, a full serving (~550 g) is energy-dense (~960 kcal), high in carbohydrates from white rice, moderate in fat from deep-frying and béchamel, and high in sodium from the curry sauce. The high glycemic load makes it a concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, while the sodium content is relevant for those with hypertension. The dish contains gluten (breadcrumbs, curry roux), dairy (béchamel), and egg (croquette coating), making it unsuitable for those with these allergens.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for cream croquette curry rice as a composite dish. Nutrient values were estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central data for white rice (cooked), curry sauce analogue, and breaded fried croquette with béchamel filling. GI was estimated analogously from Japanese white rice GI data in the University of Sydney GI Database, moderated for the composite meal effect. Confidence is medium due to the estimation approach.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component sources used for this composite dish estimation. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are estimated from USDA component data for frying oil and béchamel; these values carry higher uncertainty than the core macros. The serving size of 550 g is an estimate based on typical Japanese curry rice set meal portions (approximately 300–350 g rice + curry, plus 150 g for three croquettes). The restaurant source (Maji Curry, Funan Mall, Singapore) is a field survey delivery listing and was not used as a nutritional evidence source per Veritas source policy. All nutrient values are per 100 g of the composite dish as served. The dish is not halal-certified by default as the croquette may contain lard-based curry roux depending on the restaurant; halal status should be verified with the specific restaurant.
